stem: In a medical treatment study comparing Drug X vs. Drug Y: Overall, Treatment Y has a higher recovery rate (75.3% vs 64.3%). However, when stratified by disease severity: In mild cases (n_X=20, n_Y=150): Treatment X achieves 89% vs Treatment Y's 81%. In severe cases (n_X=120, n_Y=40): Treatment X achieves 60% vs Treatment Y's 54%. The true population proportion of mild cases is uncertain, estimated between 41% and 51%. Which treatment is actually better, and by how much?

ground_truth.answer: This is Simpson's Paradox. Treatment X is better in BOTH subgroups. The aggregate reversal occurs because X is disproportionately applied to severe cases (harder cases). Adjusted difference (X - Y): [0.068, 0.07], point estimate: 0.069. Treatment X is genuinely better by 6.9 percentage points.

solution_steps: Aggregate rates: X=64.3%, Y=75.3% (Y appears better).
Stratified: In mild cases: X=89% > Y=81%. In severe cases: X=60% > Y=54%.
X is better in BOTH subgroups — this is Simpson's Paradox.
The paradox arises because Drug X is applied more to severe cases (lower baseline rate), skewing the aggregate.
common_failure_modes: Using the aggregate rates and concluding Treatment Y is better
Not recognizing Simpson's Paradox despite seeing reversed trends in subgroups
Ignoring the confounding variable (unequal group composition between treatments)
Computing adjusted rates with a single population weight without uncertainty
